edges.description: Dysregulated CD4+ T cell subsets provide help to autoreactive B cells within germinal centers, driving aPL-specific B cell responses.
edges.source: CD4+ T cell dysregulation and autoreactive B cell licensing
edges.target: Autoantibody generation against beta2GPI and phospholipid complexes

edges.description: Circulating aPL bind beta2GPI and phospholipid-associated targets on vascular and placental surfaces.
edges.source: Autoantibody generation against beta2GPI and phospholipid complexes

edges.description: Surface-bound immune complexes engage receptors such as TLR4 and trigger downstream inflammatory signaling cascades.
Intracellular signaling induces a proadhesive and procoagulant endothelial phenotype.
Signaling in monocytes induces tissue factor expression and amplifies coagulation.
Signaling promotes platelet activation and thromboxane-driven thrombus propagation.
Activated endothelium amplifies local inflammatory and complement cascades.
Activated endothelium promotes coagulation and vascular occlusion.
Monocyte-derived tissue factor accelerates coagulation and promotes thrombus formation.
Activated platelets reinforce thrombus growth and stabilize vascular occlusion.
Complement-linked inflammatory hemostasis shifts clot remodeling toward persistence.
Complement deposition at the maternal-fetal interface promotes placental inflammation and perfusion failure.
Failure to lyse fibrin-rich clots contributes to recurrent thrombosis in arteries, veins, and microvessels.
Vascular occlusion reduces tissue perfusion and causes cumulative neurologic, renal, pulmonary, and other organ injury.
Diffuse small-vessel occlusion can escalate into thrombotic microangiopathy with acute multiorgan failure.
Placental dysfunction leads to miscarriage, preeclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and preterm delivery.

nodes.description: Recurrent venous, arterial, and small-vessel thrombosis is the central clinical consequence of APS procoagulant signaling.

evidence.reference_title: Antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The antiphospholipid syndrome causes venous, arterial, and small-vessel thrombosis; pregnancy loss; and preterm delivery for patients with severe pre-eclampsia or placental insufficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Risk factors for arterial thrombosis in antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is associated with the risk of both arterial and venous thrombosis.

nodes.description: Adaptive immune responses generate antiphospholipid antibodies that bind beta2 glycoprotein I or negatively charged phospholipids.
nodes.id: Autoantibody generation against beta2GPI and phospholipid complexes

evidence.reference_title: Cellular and Molecular Mechanisms of Anti-Phospholipid Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The primary anti-phospholipid syndrome (APS) is characterized by the production of antibodies that bind the phospholipid-binding protein β2 glycoprotein I (β2GPI) or that directly recognize negatively charged membrane phospholipids in a manner that may contribute to arterial or venous thrombosis.

evidence.reference_title: B cells in primary antiphospholipid syndrome: Review and remaining challenges.
evidence.snippet: It is now widely accepted that antiphospholipid antibodies (aPL) have direct pathogenic effects and that B cells, notably through aPL production, play a key role in the development of antiphospholipid syndrome (APS).

nodes.description: Dysregulation of CD4+ T cell subsets (helper, regulatory, and follicular) breaks immune tolerance and licenses autoreactive B cells to produce pathogenic antiphospholipid antibodies.
nodes.id: CD4+ T cell dysregulation and autoreactive B cell licensing

evidence.reference_title: Dysregulation of helper, regulatory, and follicular CD4(+) T cells in primary antiphospholipid syndrome: Immunopathology and implications for targeted immunomodulatory therapy.
evidence.snippet: APS is skewed toward Th1 and Th17 polarization, diminished Treg frequency or function, and a dysregulated Tfh/Tfr axis. Specifically, increased activated Tfh cells alongside reduced Tfr cells and skewing toward Tfh17-like subsets, have been linked to increased aPL titres and disease burden.

evidence.reference_title: Dysregulation of helper, regulatory, and follicular CD4(+) T cells in primary antiphospholipid syndrome: Immunopathology and implications for targeted immunomodulatory therapy.
evidence.snippet: the cumulative data support a model in which APS involves a coordinated disruption of CD4+ T cell homeostasis, encompassing both effector and regulatory arms.

nodes.description: Catastrophic APS represents diffuse small-vessel thrombosis with thrombotic microangiopathy, hematologic injury, and acute multiorgan failure.

evidence.reference_title: [Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome].
evidence.snippet: Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome is the most dangerous form of the antiphospholipid syndrome, which is characterized by rapid onset of thrombosis in small vessels of many organs and intravascular coagulation, thrombocytopenia and hemolytic anemia.

evidence.reference_title: [Catastrophic antiphospholipid syndrome].
evidence.snippet: The syndrome develops over a short period of time with acute multi-organ failure, including kidney, respiratory, cardiovascular, central nervous system and adrenal glands, often associated with disseminated thrombotic microangiopathy.

nodes.description: aPL-beta2GPI complexes accumulate on endothelial, monocyte, platelet, and placental surfaces where they can engage pathogenic receptor systems.

evidence.reference_title: Cellular and Molecular Mechanisms of Anti-Phospholipid Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Clinically, the binding of antibodies to β2GPI could contribute to pathogenesis by formation of immune complexes or modification of coagulation steps that operate along cell surfaces.

nodes.description: Complement cascade activation amplifies vascular inflammation, thrombosis, and placental injury in APS.

evidence.reference_title: Antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Complement activation might have a central pathogenetic role.

nodes.description: aPL signaling converts endothelial cells into a proadhesive, inflammatory, and procoagulant vascular surface.

evidence.reference_title: Antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Antiphospholipid antibodies promote activation of endothelial cells, monocytes, and platelets; and overproduction of tissue factor and thromboxane A2.

nodes.description: Reduced fibrin clearance allows APS-associated clots to persist, propagate, and recur.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired Fibrinolysis in the Antiphospholipid Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Impaired fibrinolysis has been found in APS patients with thrombotic as well as obstetric manifestations.

nodes.description: aPL-beta2GPI complexes activate TLR4-p38 MAPK-NFkB and related intracellular pathways in susceptible target cells.

evidence.reference_title: Examining how antiphospholipid antibodies activate intracellular signaling pathways: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: Diverse experimental evidence exists implicating the activation of various different cell surface receptors and intracellular pathways by antiphospholipid antibodies (aPL).

evidence.reference_title: Examining how antiphospholipid antibodies activate intracellular signaling pathways: a systematic review.
evidence.snippet: TLR4, p38 MAPK and NFκB are involved in mediating pathogenic effects of aPL on different cell types and may be potential therapeutic targets in antiphospholipid syndrome.

nodes.description: Activated monocytes upregulate tissue factor and intensify thrombin generation.

evidence.reference_title: Antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Antiphospholipid antibodies promote activation of endothelial cells, monocytes, and platelets; and overproduction of tissue factor and thromboxane A2.

nodes.description: Recurrent thrombotic occlusion causes cumulative injury across the brain, kidneys, lungs, and other organs.

evidence.reference_title: Morbidity, mortality, and organ damage in patients with antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: After a mean followup of 7.55 years, 29% of patients experienced organ damage and 5 died... Neurologic damage is the most common cause of morbidity.

evidence.reference_title: Renal involvement in primary antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The kidney is a major target organ in both primary and secondary antiphospholipid syndrome... APSN is a vascular nephropathy characterized by small vessel vaso-occlusive lesions.

evidence.reference_title: [Venous thromboembolism in antiphosholipid syndrome].
evidence.snippet: Venous thromboembolism belongs to the most frequent clinical manifestation of this syndrome... we summarised basic pathophysiological mechanisms of venous thrombosis and lung embolism development.

evidence.reference_title: Risk factors for damage accrual in primary antiphospholipid syndrome: A retrospective single-center cohort study.
evidence.snippet: APS patients had a higher frequency of damage accrual. Microangiopathy and non-criteria manifestations were independent risk factors for damage accrual.

nodes.description: At the maternal-fetal interface, aPL-driven inflammation, trophoblast dysfunction, and impaired placental perfusion produce the obstetric branch of APS.

evidence.reference_title: Mechanisms of antiphospholipid antibody-associated pregnancy complications.
evidence.snippet: Recent clinical and experimental observations suggest that the pathophysiology of pregnancy failure in patients with APS may involve inflammation at the maternal-fetal interface and disruption of normal trophoblast function and survival, rather than a pro-thrombotic event.

evidence.reference_title: Predictors of pregnancy outcome in antiphospholipid syndrome: a review.
evidence.snippet: It provides a non-invasive method for the study of uteroplacental blood flow, being able to detect a condition of impaired placental perfusion, due to the presence of circulating antiphospholipid antibodies (aPL).

nodes.description: Platelets exposed to aPL signaling become activated and generate thromboxane-dependent prothrombotic amplification.

evidence.reference_title: Antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: Antiphospholipid antibodies promote activation of endothelial cells, monocytes, and platelets; and overproduction of tissue factor and thromboxane A2.

evidence.reference_title: Impaired Fibrinolysis in the Antiphospholipid Syndrome.
evidence.snippet: aPL induce excessive activation of the endothelium, monocytes, and platelets in consort with aberrations in hemostasis/clotting, fibrinolytic system, and complement activation.

nodes.description: Placental insufficiency in APS leads to recurrent miscarriage, preeclampsia, fetal growth restriction, and preterm delivery.

evidence.reference_title: Antiphospholipid syndrome.
evidence.snippet: The antiphospholipid syndrome causes venous, arterial, and small-vessel thrombosis; pregnancy loss; and preterm delivery for patients with severe pre-eclampsia or placental insufficiency.

evidence.reference_title: Predictors of pregnancy outcome in antiphospholipid syndrome: a review.
evidence.snippet: In pregnant women, antiphospholipid syndrome (APS) is associated with an increased risk of preeclampsia, fetal intrauterine growth restriction, and other complications related to uteroplacental insufficiency.
